name: QA and Automation - MERN Stack
scope: A person with BASIC proficiency in QA and Automation for the MERN stack should be able to explain the role of automated testing across a typical MERN application (MongoDB, Express, React, Node.js), the difference between unit, integration, and end-to-end tests, and when each layer is appropriate. They can author and run tests using Jest as the test runner, write React component tests with React Testing Library using user-facing queries (getByRole, getByLabelText, getByText) and the async findBy* family, and write Express integration tests with Supertest that exercise real HTTP endpoints. They understand basic test structure (describe/it, AAA, beforeEach/afterEach), test isolation, and the importance of deterministic test data — particularly for database-backed tests where they can use mongodb-memory-server or per-test cleanup. They can mock the network layer (using MSW or jest.mock for fetch/axios) so frontend tests do not depend on a live backend, and stub Mongo or external services in backend tests when appropriate. They use userEvent to simulate user interactions, handle async UI state with findBy / waitFor instead of fixed sleeps, and understand fake timers for code that depends on setTimeout/setInterval. They distinguish between rendering bugs, data bugs, and test bugs, read failure messages and stack traces, and can fix the typical sources of flakiness (shared state, brittle queries, missing await, unmocked network). They follow basic conventions for test file location (__tests__/, *.test.ts), keep tests focused on one behaviour, and run their suite locally and in CI.
long_scope:
{"sections": [{"name": "QA Foundations in a MERN Codebase", "scope_included": "- Understands the test pyramid as it applies to a MERN app: many unit tests, fewer integration tests, a small number of E2E checks
- Understands the difference between testing a React component, testing an Express route handler, and end-to-end testing the whole stack
- Understands what is meant by deterministic vs flaky tests and why determinism matters in CI
- Understands the AAA structure (Arrange, Act, Assert) and applies it consistently in test code
- Understands the lifecycle of a test in Jest: describe / it / hooks (beforeAll, beforeEach, afterEach, afterAll) and what scope each hook applies to
- Understands test isolation: that no test should depend on the state left behind by a previous test
- Knows how Jest discovers tests (file patterns, testMatch, testEnvironment) at a basic level
- Understands the role of CI in running the test suite and how a failing test should block a merge
- Understands at a basic level why coverage by itself is not a quality signal", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "React Component Testing with React Testing Library", "scope_included": "- Understands the React Testing Library philosophy: tests should resemble how the user uses the component, not its internal structure
- Uses the recommended query priority: getByRole > getByLabelText > getByPlaceholderText > getByText > getByTestId, and avoids querying by CSS class or DOM structure
- Distinguishes getBy*, queryBy*, and findBy*: when each is appropriate (assert exists, assert NOT exists, assert eventually exists)
- Uses userEvent (not raw fireEvent) for user-like interactions: typing, clicking, selecting, tabbing
- Handles asynchronous UI correctly with findBy* and waitFor instead of sleeps
- Understands act() warnings and what they mean (state updates outside an act-wrapped call) and how to avoid them in normal code
- Renders components with the testing library render() helper and uses screen.* for queries by default
- Understands how to provide context providers (Router, Theme, QueryClient) in a wrapper for components under test
- Tests both presentational components and small connected components, including basic form interactions and validation errors", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Express Backend Testing with Jest and Supertest", "scope_included": "- Understands how to expose an Express app instance separately from app.listen so it can be loaded by Supertest in tests
- Uses Supertest to make HTTP-level assertions against an Express app: status codes, response body fields, headers
- Writes integration tests that exercise route handler + middleware together, asserting the observable HTTP behaviour
- Knows when to write a route-level integration test versus a pure unit test of a helper function
- Handles request bodies (JSON, form-encoded), query strings, route params, and authenticated requests in test setup
- Understands middleware ordering implications (body parser, auth, cookies) when reproducing them in tests
- Asserts both happy-path responses (200/201) and error paths (400/401/404/409/500) the API actually documents
- Understands the difference between asserting on the wire response and asserting on internal state changes (DB rows)", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "MongoDB-Backed Test Data and Isolation", "scope_included": "- Understands why hitting a shared dev MongoDB from tests is a flake source and how to avoid it
- Uses mongodb-memory-server (or an equivalent in-process Mongo) to stand up a clean Mongo instance per test run, suite, or test as appropriate
- Implements per-test cleanup using beforeEach / afterEach to reset collections so no test depends on another
- Understands the trade-off between resetting the entire DB versus just the affected collections and chooses appropriately
- Seeds test data through Mongoose model APIs or direct collection inserts as the codebase prefers, in a fixture-style helper
- Understands the difference between schema-level validation errors and route-level error responses and writes tests accordingly
- Recognises common DB-pollution bugs: forgotten cleanup hook, shared singleton connection, parallel tests sharing the same DB name
- Knows the basics of Mongoose connection lifecycle (connect, disconnect) inside a test runner so the process exits cleanly", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Mocking the Network Layer in Frontend Tests", "scope_included": "- Understands why a React component test that hits a real backend is fragile and inappropriate as a unit/integration test
- Uses MSW (Mock Service Worker) to intercept fetch / axios calls at the network layer so tests do not depend on a real server
- Understands the difference between MSW handlers defined globally for a suite and overridden per test for error paths
- Alternatively uses jest.mock on a module (e.g. mocking an api/client.ts file) when MSW is not yet wired up
- Asserts on what the UI shows after a mocked response, not on internal call counts when avoidable
- Handles loading state, success state, and error state coverage for a component that fetches data
- Understands the difference between mocking the network and mocking the imported function (and the trade-offs)
- Recognises the smell of a test that mocks too much and effectively asserts the mock back to itself", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Async, Timers, and Reliability Patterns", "scope_included": "- Replaces hard-coded sleeps (setTimeout, jest.advanceTimers without intent, page.waitForTimeout-like patterns) with condition-based waits
- Uses await findBy* and waitFor for UI that updates after a network call resolves
- Understands jest.useFakeTimers / runAllTimers and when faking timers is appropriate (debounce, polling) versus harmful (real network)
- Awaits all asynchronous calls correctly (returns promises from test bodies, awaits userEvent calls)
- Recognises common flakiness sources: missing await, race between unmount and state set, shared module-level state, leaked timers
- Uses retries and timeouts at the Jest level only as a last resort and prefers fixing the root cause
- Understands the difference between a flaky test caused by the application and one caused by the test", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Quality, Maintainability and Review", "scope_included": "- Writes tests that are readable, with intent-revealing names like 'shows error when login fails' rather than 'test1'
- Keeps each test focused on one behaviour and avoids testing several unrelated things in one it block
- Avoids duplication by extracting small render helpers, fixture builders, and request helpers, but does not over-abstract
- Reviews other people's tests for the common smells: brittle queries, missing await, mocked-back-to-itself assertions, leaked DB state
- Updates tests when component or API behaviour changes intentionally, instead of leaving stale snapshots or skipped tests
- Documents non-obvious test setup briefly when it would surprise a future reader
- Communicates test failures clearly: which behaviour broke, what reproduction steps, and whether the bug is in the app or the test", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Tooling, Project Setup and CI", "scope_included": "- Reads and makes basic edits to a Jest config (testEnvironment, setupFiles, transform, moduleNameMapper) when guided
- Knows how to run a single test file or a single test by name from the CLI for fast iteration
- Understands how a typical MERN repo organises tests (__tests__/ folder, *.test.ts colocated with source) and follows the existing convention
- Understands at a basic level how the test suite is wired into CI and how a failing test blocks a PR
- Reads Jest output (failure stacks, diff snapshots, console logs) to identify the failing line and probable cause
- Knows how to update or invalidate Jest module caches when a test starts behaving unexpectedly after a refactor", "is_non_core_section": true}, {"name": "E2E and Cross-Layer Testing (Awareness Only)", "scope_included": "- Knows that E2E tools like Cypress and Playwright exist and what they cover compared to component / integration tests
- Understands at a high level when a behaviour is better verified end-to-end (e.g. cross-page flow, real auth integration) versus at a lower layer
- Does NOT need to author or maintain a full E2E suite at this proficiency level — that is owned by more senior automation engineers", "is_non_core_section": true}]}
